before: Ella Fitzgerald Sings the Cole Porter Song Book is a 1956 studio double album by American jazz singer Ella Fitzgerald, accompanied by a studio orchestra conducted and arranged by Buddy Bregman, focusing on the songs of Cole Porter. It is the first in a series of thematic LPs devoted to great American songwriters that Fitzgerald recorded from 1956 to 1964.
after: Ella Loves Cole is a 1972 album by Ella Fitzgerald, released on Atlantic. Its program includes "I Get a Kick Out of You", "I've Got You Under My Skin", and "Love for Sale".
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release: title, year (releaseGroup.firstReleaseDate 1972), label (Atlantic), tracklist

before: Mingus is an album by the jazz bassist and composer Charles Mingus. The album was recorded in October and November 1960 in New York and released in late 1961 on Nat Hentoff's Candid label.
after: Four Trombones is a 1957 album on the Debut label. The multi-leader session is credited to Kai Winding, J.J. Johnson, Bennie Green, Willie Dennis, John Lewis, and Charlie Mingus, and includes "Now's the Time", "Trombosphere", and "Ow!".
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release: title, year (1957), label (Debut), artist credits, tracklist

before: "Grazing in the Grass" is an instrumental composed by Philemon Hou and first recorded by the South African trumpeter Hugh Masekela. Released in the United States as a single in 1968, it followed United States trumpeter Herb Alpert's vocal performance of "This Guy's in Love with You" to the top spot on the Hot 100 chart, ranking it as the 18th biggest hit of the year. The song also reached No. 15 Adult Contemporary. Masekela included the song in his albums Grazing in the Grass: The Best of Hugh Masekela (2001), Still Grazing (2004), and Live at the Market Theatre (2006). The biggest crossover hit in African jazz history. Masekela became an ambassador for South African music worldwide.
after: Hugh Masekela's Grazin' is a 1968 album by Hugh Masekela, released on Uni. Its tracks include "Grazing in the Grass" and "Bajabula Bonke".
evidenceUsed: ours catalog fields only (title, artist, year 1968, label Uni, keyTracks); MusicBrainz returned no release and the stored wiki extract is the song, not the album

before: Out of the Loop is the seventh and final studio album by the Brecker Brothers that was released by GRP Records in 1994. In 1995 the album won the brothers two Grammy Awards for Best Contemporary Jazz Performance and Best Instrumental Composition.
after: Hearts and Numbers is a 1985 album released on Hip Pocket Records, credited to Don Grolnick and Michael Brecker. Its tracks include "Pointing at the Moon", "Pools", and "The Four Sleepers".
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release: title, year (1985), label (Hip Pocket Records), artist credits (Don Grolnick, Michael Brecker), tracklist

before: Herbie Hancock Trio is an album by Herbie Hancock released on September 21, 1977, in Japan. It includes performances with bassist Ron Carter and drummer Tony Williams. A second selection of six tracks recorded by the trio during the same day's sessions was released under Ron Carter's name as Third Plane. Williams first gained fame as a member of Miles Davis's "Second Great Quintet", and later pioneered jazz fusion with Davis's group and his own combo, The Tony Williams Lifetime.
after: Herbie Hancock Trio with Ron Carter + Tony Williams is a 1981 album released in Japan on CBS/Sony. The trio of Herbie Hancock, Ron Carter, and Tony Williams plays "Stablemates", "Dolphin Dance", and "That Old Black Magic".
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release: title, year (1981), country (JP), label (CBS/Sony), artist credits, tracklist

before: Night Song is an album by the saxophonist Arthur Blythe, released via Clarity Recordings in 1997. This Enja Records date features "Dear Dessa" as part of Arthur Blythe's free jazz output from 1991.
after: Hipmotism is a 1991 album by Arthur Blythe, released on Enja. Its tracks include "Dear Dessa", "Dance Benita Dance", and the title track "Hipmotism".

before: Infinity is a studio album by jazz trumpeter Tom Harrell, released by HighNote Records in March 2019. Features "Jericho" and "Haiku" in a contemporary jazz context, highlighting Tom Harrell's interpretive range.
after: Is That So? is a 2014 album released on Stunt Records, credited to Tom Harrell. Its tracks include "Jericho", "Haiku", and "Hot House".
evidenceUsed: ours catalog fields (artist Tom Harrell, year 2014, label Stunt Records) plus MusicBrainz tracklist; MB release credits Niels Vincentz so entity match is unsure — artist kept from ours, no personnel asserted

before: Terence Blanchard is an album by American jazz trumpeter Terence Blanchard. This is his debut full-length album as a leader. The record was released on June 18, 1991, via Columbia Records.
after: Jazz in Film is a 1999 album by Terence Blanchard, released on Sony Classical. Its program includes "A Streetcar Named Desire", "Chinatown", and "Anatomy of a Murder".

before: Amanecer is an album by Joey Calderazzo. It was recorded in 2006 and released by Marsalis Music. He played extensively in bands led by Michael Brecker and Branford Marsalis, and has also led his own bands.
after: Joey Calderazzo is a 2000 self-titled album by Joey Calderazzo, released on Columbia. Its tracks include "The Oracle", "Haiku", and "Time Remembered".

before: Kenny Burrell is an album by guitarist Kenny Burrell recorded in 1957 and released on the Prestige label. Guitarist Kenny Burrell's hard bop sensibility is on display in this 1956 session, a document of the hard bop era.
after: Kenny Burrell is a 1956 self-titled album by Kenny Burrell, released on Blue Note (BLP 1543). Its tracks include "Get Happy", "But Not for Me", and "Moten Swing".

before: The Art Ensemble of Chicago is an avant-garde jazz group that grew out of the Association for the Advancement of Creative Musicians (AACM) in the late 1960s. The ensemble integrates many jazz styles and plays many instruments, including "little instruments": bells, bicycle horns, birthday party noisemakers, wind chimes, and various forms of percussion. The musicians would wear costumes and face paint while performing. These characteristics combined to make the ensemble's performances both aural and visual. While playing in Europe in 1969, five hundred instruments were used. Showed the Art Ensemble's range and their ability to work in commercial contexts without compromising. 'Theme de Yoyo' became a rare avant-garde dance floor hit.
after: Les Stances à Sophie is a 1970 soundtrack album by the Art Ensemble of Chicago. Its tracks include "Thème de Yoyo", "Thème de Céline", and "Thème amour universal".
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release: title, year (1970), releaseGroup secondaryType (Soundtrack), artist (Art Ensemble of Chicago), tracklist; label omitted (ours Pathe vs MB Nessa pressing, unresolved)

before: "Lover Man " is a 1941 popular song written by Jimmy Davis, Roger ("Ram") Ramirez, and James Sherman. It is particularly associated with Billie Holiday, for whom it was written, and her version was inducted into the Grammy Hall of Fame in 1989.
after: Lover Man is a 1951 album by Billie Holiday, released on Decca. Its tracks include "Lover Man (Oh, Where Can You Be?)", "Crazy He Calls Me", and "Solitude".
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release: title, year (1951), label (Decca Records), artist (Billie Holiday), tracklist; stored wiki is a disambiguation page (wrong entity), ignored

before: Ella and Duke at the Cote D'Azur is a 1967 live album by Ella Fitzgerald, accompanied by the big band of Duke Ellington. Captures pianist Duke Ellington in a None swing setting, adding to the recorded documentation of the swing era.
after: Lullaby of Birdland is a jazz album whose program includes "Lullaby of Birdland", "Anthropology", and "Ask Me Now".
evidenceUsed: ours.keyTracks only; both MusicBrainz and the stored wiki identify the release as a Lee Konitz / Barry Harris date, contradicting ours.artist (Duke Ellington), so neither is entity-matched — no leader, date, or label asserted
